title: For FLP scholars, little things mean a lot
primary_topics: FLP scholars passing the 2019 bar exam
'little things mean a lot' philanthropic philosophy
sub_topics: Leo Francis Abot (ADMU summa cum laude, class valedictorian)
John Anthony Almerino (USC magna, class valedictorian)
Arvin Paolo Cortez (UP cum laude → ADMU)
King Anthony Perez (UCebu valedictorian, journalism background)
Ma. Vida Malaya Villarico (PUP class top, UP Community Development)
Micah Celine Carpio (DLSU valedictorian, marketing magna)
Alimar Mohammad Malabad (Ibanag youth leader, SBU)
Dion Ceazar Pascua (FEU accounting magna, SBU law)
Tess Marie Tan (USC, Stetson Moot Court world champion)

stances.claim: The peering questions of the board of judges, the midnight toil of the screening panel, the scholarship prizes from donors, and bits of learning on liberty and prosperity were 'little things' that meant a lot in inspiring the scholars' stories of faith in passing the bar.

These FLP-TYKF scholars — coming from CPAs, farmer's sons, working students, broadcast journalists, debaters, mooters, and cultural-preservation leaders — represent 'the future leaders of our country' and demonstrate the integrative formation FLP aims to cultivate.
Listening to scholars' stories 'uplifted [him] no end as they reminded [him] of [his] own youth' — implicit identification of his own working-student poverty journey with theirs.

institutions: Stetson International Environmental Law Moot Court

signature_phrases: the future leaders of our country
they reminded me of my own youth
the king's good servant but God's first
register_markers: title-phrase recurrence ('little things must have meant a lot')
decision_framework_signals: applies 'little things mean a lot' framework to evaluate philanthropic intervention value
uses structured-biographical celebration as transmission mechanism for institutional values
frames implicit autobiographical resonance (his own working-student years) as authority for endorsing similar journeys
notable_anecdotes: Jun Dexter Rojas, a farmer's son, working for several years after his UP bachelor's to help his siblings get to college before enrolling at PUP and becoming its first bar topper
Tess Marie Tan being the first Filipino woman to be acclaimed 'World Champion and Best Over-all Oralist' in the 21-year history of the Stetson International Environmental Law Moot Court Competition
